chunk_summary: Guidelines for screening preterm infants under six months emphasize using corrected age and appropriate growth charts to distinguish prematurity-related size from malnutrition. Transition to WHO standards post-term and manage severe acute malnutrition based on clinical signs, regardless of preterm status.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of corrected age in assessing growth for preterm infants.
Learn how to select and transition between growth charts for preterm and term-equivalent infants.
Identify clinical signs of severe acute malnutrition in preterm infants and appropriate management protocols.
key_facts: Corrected age = chronological age - (40 weeks - gestational age at birth).
Use preterm growth references (e.g., INTERGROWTH-21st) until term-equivalent age, then transition to WHO Child Growth Standards.
Avoid switching growth charts between visits to prevent false centile crossing.
MUAC thresholds for severe acute malnutrition in infants may include <110 mm at 6 weeks.
Prematurity explains small size but not nutritional oedema, persistent weight loss, or clinical compromise.
reference_quote: Use corrected age when interpreting growth and development in ex-preterm infants, because chronological age overestimates expected growth for gestation. (Source: WHO guideline on the prevention and management of wasting and nutritional oedema in infants and children under 5 years)
document_key_topics: Growth chart selection for preterm infants
Severe acute malnutrition criteria in preterm infants
Transitioning to WHO Child Growth Standards post-term
